summary:
The Zarzian culture was an Epipaleolithic hunter-gatherer tradition of the Zagros Mountains region, encompassing parts of modern Iran, Iraq, and adjacent territories, dated to approximately 18,000–8,000 BCE. Named after the Zarzi Cave in the Sulaymaniyah region of the Kurdistan Region of Iraq, the culture is defined by a microlithic stone tool industry characterized by small geometric flints including crescents, triangles, and backed bladelets used as components of composite tools and weapons.

Zarzian communities were mobile hunter-gatherers exploiting the upland resources of the Zagros Mountains — hunting wild sheep, goat, pig, and red deer in the oak woodland and grassland environments of the mountain foothills. Their sites are typically small cave and rock shelter occupations reflecting seasonal mobility. The Zarzian culture is significant as the local cultural tradition immediately preceding the transition to agriculture in the Zagros, which eventually produced one of the world's major independent centers of animal domestication. The [[event:1258|domestication of goats]] and the early management of sheep in the Zagros foothills during the early Neolithic period occurred within communities whose cultural antecedents lay in the Zarzian tradition. The [[ event:1267|Shanidar Cave]] sequence in Iraq documents Zarzian occupation of a key reference site for the terminal Pleistocene and early Holocene in this region.
